title: Embeddings
lesson:
A computer cannot do maths on the word dog. It can only do maths on numbers. So the first job inside any language model is to turn each token (the small chunk of text a model reads) into a list of numbers. That list of numbers is called an embedding. An embedding is just a long row of decimal numbers — say 768 or 4,096 of them — that stands in for a piece of meaning. The clever part is which numbers get used: the model learns to place words with similar meanings close together, and unrelated words far apart.

The map-of-cities analogy

Imagine every word printed as a pin on a giant map. On a real map, two numbers — latitude and longitude — fix any city's position, and cities near each other tend to be similar (same country, similar weather). An embedding is the same idea, just with far more than two numbers. Each extra number is another dimension (one axis of measurement, like an extra direction you can travel). With hundreds of dimensions, the model has enough room to put king near queen, Paris near London, and banana way off in the fruit district. We can't picture 768 directions at once, but the maths works exactly like distance on a map: close pins mean related meanings.

Meaning becomes direction

Here is the part that feels like magic but is really just arithmetic. Because meaning is stored as position, you can do maths on meaning. The famous example: take the embedding for king, subtract man, add woman, and you land almost exactly on queen. The direction you travelled encoded the idea of "royalty" and "gender" as movements through the space. You don't program this in — the model discovers it while training on huge amounts of text, simply by noticing which words show up in similar company. This is the old saying made literal: "you shall know a word by the company it keeps."

How we measure "close"

To compare two embeddings, we rarely use plain straight-line distance. The standard tool is cosine similarity — it measures the angle between two number-rows, ignoring how long they are. Think of two arrows pointing out from the centre of the map: if they point the same way, they're similar (score near 1); at right angles, unrelated (score near 0); opposite directions, opposite meaning (score near -1). Angle is used instead of distance because it cares about direction of meaning, not loudness or length.

Two kinds of embedding (don't mix them up)

Beginners trip here, so let's separate them clearly. A token embedding lives inside the model and represents one token, before context is added. A sentence (or document) embedding is a single number-row that summarises a whole chunk of text into one vector you can store and search later.

Token embeddingSentence/document embedding
Represents one tokenRepresents a whole sentence or passage
Lives inside the model's first layerOutput of a dedicated embedding model
You rarely touch it directlyYou store it and search over it
Feeds the TransformerPowers Semantic Search and RAG

Why this matters in real products

The whole field of Semantic Search runs on embeddings: instead of matching keywords, you embed the user's question, embed every document, and return the closest ones by cosine similarity. So a search for "how do I cancel" can find a help article titled "ending your subscription" even though they share no words. This is also the engine room of RAG (feeding a model your own documents) and the reason Vector Databases exist — they are built to store millions of embeddings and find the nearest ones fast.

Here is the everyday way you'd create one with a hosted API:

from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

resp = client.embeddings.create(
    model="text-embedding-3-small",
    input="How do I cancel my plan?",
)
vec = resp.data[0].embedding   # a list of ~1536 floats
print(len(vec), vec[:3])       # 1536 [0.013, -0.021, 0.004]

And the comparison itself is tiny — no model needed once you have the vectors:

import numpy as np

def cosine(a, b):
    a, b = np.array(a), np.array(b)
    return a @ b / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))

print(cosine(vec_question, vec_article))  # e.g. 0.83 = very related

One caution: an embedding is only meaningful next to other embeddings made by the same model. The numbers from one model and another aren't comparable, the same way you can't read coordinates from a London map onto a Paris map. So pick one embedding model and stick to it for everything you compare. For how text becomes tokens in the first place, see Tokenization.
mentalModel: An embedding is a pin on a giant invisible map of meaning. Words and sentences with similar meanings sit close together, so finding "related" becomes just measuring which pins are nearby.
mistakes: Confusing embeddings with tokens. Tokens are the chopped-up text; embeddings are the lists of numbers that represent that text's meaning. Tokenizing comes first, embedding comes second.

Comparing vectors from two different embedding models. Each model builds its own private map, so a vector from one is gibberish next to a vector from another. Use one model consistently for everything you search.
Re-embedding the same text constantly. Embeddings are stable for a given model and input, so cache them. Calling the API for an unchanged document every search wastes money and time.

Using straight-line distance when cosine similarity is expected. Most embedding models are tuned for angle-based similarity. Mixing measures (or forgetting to normalise) can quietly tank your search quality.

Embedding chunks that are too big or too small. A whole 50-page document in one vector blurs every topic together; a single word loses context. Sensible Chunking is what makes retrieval actually work.

Assuming more dimensions always means better. A 3072-dimension vector costs more storage and search time than a 768 one, often for tiny quality gains. Match the size to the job, not to the biggest number available.
exercise:
Use any embedding API (OpenAI, Cohere, or a local model via Hugging Face) to embed five short phrases: cat, kitten, dog, car, and banana. Compute the cosine similarity between every pair. Confirm that cat and kitten score highest, that cat beats cat-vs-car (a near spelling but distant meaning), and that banana sits far from the animals.

Stretch goal: Build a tiny semantic search. Embed 10 sentences of your own text, store the vectors in a list, then embed a fresh query and return the top 2 most similar sentences by cosine. Note when it correctly finds a match that shares no keywords with your query.
solution: You should see cat↔kitten highest (often ~0.8+), cat↔dog moderately high (both animals/pets), and cat↔car low despite the similar spelling — proof the model encodes meaning, not letters. banana should be the odd one out, far from all the animals. For the stretch goal, the magic moment is a query like "my pet meowed" surfacing a stored sentence about a cat even with zero shared words: that is semantic search working. Tips that catch people out: embed the query with the same model as the stored sentences, and normalise vectors (or just use cosine) so length differences don't skew results.
summary: An embedding turns a piece of text into a long list of numbers that captures its meaning as a position on an invisible map. Similar meanings land close together, so we measure relatedness with cosine similarity (the angle between vectors). Token embeddings live inside the model; sentence/document embeddings are what you store and search. They power semantic search, RAG, and vector databases — just always compare vectors from the same model.

keyTerms.def: A list of numbers that represents a piece of text (or other data) as a point in a meaning-space, where similar meanings sit close together.
Another word for that list of numbers. A 768-dimension embedding is just a vector with 768 entries.
One number (one axis) in an embedding. More dimensions give the model more room to separate fine shades of meaning.
A score from -1 to 1 measuring the angle between two vectors. Near 1 means very similar meaning; near 0 means unrelated.
The embedding of a single token, created inside the model's first layer before context from other words is added.
One vector that summarises a whole sentence or passage, produced by a dedicated embedding model and stored for later search.
A model whose job is to output embeddings (e.g. text-embedding-3-small). Vectors are only comparable when made by the same one.
The imaginary multi-dimensional 'map' that all of one model's embeddings live in, where distance and direction encode meaning.
